title: Evenings with Graham & Emmy
teaser: Local grid strains and farmland fights collide with the governor's new data center standards.

lines.text: Good evening, and welcome to Evenings with Graham & Emmy — the Michigan Data Wire, your podcast on Michigan's data center buildout. I'm Graham.
And I'm Emmy.
Tonight we're looking at how storms and local resistance are forcing new pauses on data centers, right as Governor Whitmer rolled out statewide rules today.
That Warren decision after Saturday's storms really highlights the pressure on aging infrastructure.

Exactly. Warren's city council approved a temporary moratorium on new data centers after July storms knocked down more than sixty power lines. City attorneys are now drafting the pause while they revise zoning rules.
What does that tell us about the grid's capacity in places like Macomb County?
It shows that even one bad storm can expose how quickly these large projects add strain. Officials are hitting pause so they can figure out stronger rules before approving more.
And over in Washtenaw County, Saline Township residents are pushing back hard against that sixteen-billion-dollar Stargate project.

Yes, folks in that small farming community of about twenty-four hundred people say the facility already under construction will drain water supplies, raise utility costs, and change the rural character of the area.
How does this fit with the governor's announcement today?

Whitmer unveiled the Michigan Affordable and Responsible Growth Action Plan, which sets new siting, power, and water standards for future data centers. Companies that skip the rules could lose state incentives.
So the state is trying to get ahead of the local backlash we've seen building.
That's the idea. The plan comes as pushback grows over electric bills, water use, and lost farmland across multiple townships.
What kind of history are we looking at here with these approvals?
Many townships approved large projects without strong noise or utility protections in place. Now residents are seeing the daily impacts and filing lawsuits or holding rallies.
Like the Dowagiac case that dropped yesterday.
Right. Residents there sued the hyperscale facility in Cass County, claiming nonstop noise is disrupting homes and daily life in the small southwest Michigan community.
And the Vicksburg protest also happened yesterday.
Dozens showed up in Kalamazoo County to highlight noise, traffic, and utility strain concerns. Organizers say more actions are coming if proposals move forward.
Meanwhile the House Speaker is holding up a bill that would create statewide guardrails.
Legislation on siting, power use, and tax breaks remains stalled. Sponsors say the delay leaves townships without tools to manage the growth.
That leaves places like Augusta Township to handle it themselves with a referendum next month.
Voters there will decide whether to allow a large project after weeks of protests over traffic, noise, and power strain near Ann Arbor.
And then there's the Oracle news from Saturday.
Saline Township lifted the four-point-eight-billion-dollar cap on incentives for the Oracle-OpenAI campus. Now the company has an unlimited tax break on a forty-three-billion-dollar project.
What does removing that cap actually mean for local taxpayers?
It risks decades of lost property-tax revenue that would have gone to schools and roads. Residents could be on the hook for the difference.
How does the new state plan address these incentive issues?
The plan ties incentives to compliance with the new siting, power, and water standards. Non-compliant projects lose access to state support.
So companies have a choice: follow the rules or go it alone without the carrots.
That's the leverage the governor is using. It also shines a light on west Michigan's first major AI campus in Kent County.
The utility has a public hearing this week to explain how it will serve that Microsoft project.
Neighbors are already asking about added strain on the local grid and water supply.
What happens next if more communities follow Warren's lead?
We could see additional moratoriums while towns rewrite zoning and wait for clearer state guidance. Lawsuits like the one in Dowagiac may also multiply.
And what about the stalled House bill?
Sponsors are hoping the governor's plan creates momentum to move it forward with bipartisan support.
For listeners wondering about attending meetings, what's on deck this week?
The utility hearing on the Kent County Microsoft campus is happening soon, and Augusta Township's referendum vote is scheduled for next month.
Those are the ones where people can actually show up and speak.
Full sources and the live map at the Michigan Data Center Tracker. We're back tomorrow.

episode_title: Warren Freezes New Data Centers After Storms; Whitmer Unveils Statewide Growth Rules
